region_of_origin: China; widely used in Singapore and East Asian cuisines

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw ginkgo nut as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for this ingredient is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw ginkgo nuts is approximately 10 nuts (about 35 g), as commonly used in Chinese dessert soups such as tau suan or barley soup.

nutrient_source.title: Ginkgo nut, raw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for ginkgo nut, raw (cr_id F09030031). Secondary cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central NDB 12023 (Nuts, ginkgo nuts, raw) for saturated fat, dietary fibre, sugar, and micronutrient values not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw ginkgo nut was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on analogy with starchy seeds and chestnuts (GI ~52–60 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283). Ginkgo nuts are starchy (37.6 g carbohydrate/100 g) with moderate dietary fibre (3.4 g/100 g), supporting a low GI estimate (GI 52 is within the low GI range of <55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (52 × 37.6 × 0.35 × (1 − 3.4/37.6)) / 100 ≈ (52 × 13.8) / 100 ≈ 7.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (7 mg/100 g) and high potassium (510 mg/100 g) with a potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 73:1, strongly supporting blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Ginkgo nuts are suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in a plain preparation (approximately 10 nuts per serving); avoid sweet soup preparations with added salt or sodium-containing seasonings that would negate the favourable sodium profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (37.6 g/100 g) with a low estimated GI (~52) and moderate dietary fibre (3.4 g/100 g); net available carbohydrate per 35 g serving is approximately 13.8 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit ginkgo nut portions to approximately 10 nuts (35 g) per serving, pair with protein or low-GI foods to blunt postprandial glucose response, and strictly avoid sweet soup preparations with added sugar which substantially increase glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.68 g/100 g), very low saturated fat (0.31 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (510 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (3.4 g/100 g) collectively support a favourable cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ginkgo nuts are suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease as a low-fat, cholesterol-free, high-potassium food; limit to approximately 10 nuts per serving and avoid preparations with added sugar, salt, or saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.31 g/100 g), vitamin C (15 mg/100 g), potassium (510 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (3.4 g/100 g) support anti-inflammatory dietary patterns.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginkgo nuts may be included in the diet of individuals with rheumatoid arthritis in cooked form (approximately 10 nuts per serving); raw ginkgo nuts must never be consumed due to ginkgotoxin content, and sweet preparations with added sugar should be avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ginkgo nut (raw) — known in Chinese as 白果 (bai guo) — is the seed of the Ginkgo biloba tree, one of the oldest living tree species. The raw nut has a pale green to ivory kernel enclosed in a hard off-white shell. The kernel has a firm, slightly waxy texture and a mild, subtly sweet flavour with a faint bitterness. In Singapore and broader Chinese culinary tradition, ginkgo nuts are a classic ingredient in tau suan (split mung bean dessert soup), barley and ginkgo nut sweet soup, and fu chok (beancurd skin) dessert soup. They are also used in savoury congee and steamed egg dishes. Raw ginkgo nuts contain ginkgotoxin (4-O-methylpyridoxine), a neurotoxin that is partially reduced but not eliminated by cooking; consumption must be limited to approximately 10 nuts per serving for adults, and raw consumption must be avoided entirely. Nutritionally, ginkgo nuts are a starchy seed providing 182 kcal/100 g, with 37.6 g carbohydrate, 4.32 g protein, and only 1.68 g fat per 100 g. They are notably high in potassium (510 mg/100 g) and very low in sodium (7 mg/100 g), making them favourable for blood pressure management. Dietary fibre is approximately 3.4 g/100 g. The estimated glycemic index is low (~52), and a standard 35 g serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 7.2. Ginkgo nuts are vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. They are classified under nuts and seeds within the meat and alternatives food group in the Singapore HPB food composition system.
common_user_queries: Are ginkgo nuts safe to eat raw?
How many ginkgo nuts can I eat per day?
Are ginkgo nuts good for diabetics?
What is bai guo used for in Chinese cooking?
Are ginkgo nuts high in carbohydrates?
Can hypertensive patients eat ginkgo nuts?
What dishes use ginkgo nuts in Singapore?
meal_context: Chinese dessert soups (tau suan, barley soup, fu chok soup)

ingredient_synonyms.bai_guo: Chinese name for ginkgo nut (白果)
ingredient_synonyms.silver_apricot: Alternative English name derived from the appearance of the nut
ingredient_synonyms.maidenhair_tree_nut: Alternative English name referencing the Ginkgo biloba (maidenhair) tree
ingredient_synonyms.ginkgo_seed: Botanical synonym used in some culinary and scientific contexts

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F09030031, confidence 0.95). Secondary micronutrient values (saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, dietary fibre, vitamin C, water) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 12023 (Nuts, ginkgo nuts, raw). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 and is documented as such.
notes: 1. Ginkgotoxin safety: Raw ginkgo nuts contain ginkgotoxin (4-O-methylpyridoxine) and must be cooked before consumption. Adults should not exceed approximately 10 nuts per serving even when cooked. Children, pregnant women, and individuals on anticoagulant therapy (e.g., warfarin) should consult a healthcare provider before consuming ginkgo nuts. 2. Negative sentinel values: vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for this entry, and USDA FoodData Central NDB 12023 does not provide complete coverage for all these fields for raw ginkgo nut as of 2024. 3. Sugar_g: USDA FoodData Central NDB 12023 reports sugars at 0 g/100 g for raw ginkgo nut; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. Vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: plant-based food with no animal-derived components; naturally zero for B12 and D. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (52, low) based on starchy seed analogy (chestnut reference) from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for raw ginkgo nut was found in approved sources. 6. Dietary fibre value of 3.4 g/100 g is sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 12023 as HPB does not report fibre for this entry. 7. Vitamin C value of 15 mg/100 g is sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 12023. 8. Saturated fat (0.31 g/100 g), MUFA (0.43 g/100 g), PUFA (0.77 g/100 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 12023. 9. Water_g (55.2 g/100 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 12023. 10. Trans_fat_g set to 0.0: naturally zero for an unprocessed plant food; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central.
